speaker: interviewer
text: December 21, 1995. Ira Kaminski is the survivor. My name is Daniel [? Sedless. ?] Tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were talking about the being wrapped up in the blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Anyway, they beat me up very badly, and I went back to work-- hardly. I was very, very upset that people could do it to us, and I would like to find something to stop them. I can't-- I decided I will not let it go by just like this. Otherwise, today they did it to me and tomorrow they will continue to do the dirty-- these dirty tricks with all Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't do, myself, anything. If I-- if I will complain for-- for any-- for the Polish people, they will just laugh for me, you know? Then I-- I had an idea. How about if I will tell-- will go to the Germans? Because the Polish people, the only thing they were afraid is for a German. Maybe I should go to anybody, to the Germans and tell them the truth. I was afraid. Depends upon who the German is because we were not allowed to have any money on us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I came to an idea. There was only over there a Meister who was a ni-- he happens to be a nice guy. What happened? He didn't hit anybody and he behaved like a human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was Meister Schneider. And I said, I'll go to him. He knows me, that I am a good worker and I'm not fooling around. I'll tell him what they did to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't even want to ask anybody if I should, if it's a good idea not, because everybody will say don't do it. Don't go to Germans because you might run into a lot of trouble if you will notify them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, how about the Ger-- [INAUDIBLE] the Pol-- the Polish people? They'll find out that you told this to the Germans and the Germans will investigate, the Polish people didn't l-- Anyway, I decided I have to. I can't let it go like this. And I went to Meister Schneider, like I said, and I told him the truth, what happened. I was walk-- walking from one hall to the other. I did my job. They came-- if I want to buy bread? Yes, I want to buy bread. Because, you know, I told them open, you know, we're not getting enough bread. I need for breakfast a piece of bread. And what happened? I told him what happened. He approached me, he wants to sell me bread, and he-- I should come in over there. And what they did, they threw the blanket on me. They took the money and the blanket away, and I-- they left me beaten up.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I'll go with you. You show me who the man is. I said, OK. Come on with me. We can go right now. I went with him to that hall where they did the dirty job. And we were looking. I was looking, because I saw only one guy who came to me, propo-- with the proposition to sell the bread, and I couldn't find him. Maybe somewhere else. I went over the hall. And I couldn't see-- maybe he must have saw-- saw me, that I'm coming with the Meister, you know, with the German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And maybe they-- he was hiding or something. I couldn't find the guy. And one thing, since then, these things never happened again. They knew that there is a Jew who is not afraid to notify even Meister Schneider. Meister Schneider did what I was asking him. He said, in case of something happens, you just tell me. I said, and I appreciate it. This was a good lesson for these wild animals, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they, what they did. They thought my-- as a matter of fact, my brother-in-law came from barrack B. He was already very, very weak, and they used to send these guys, the weak guys from the other barracks, to barrack C, and they finish them off.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your brother-in-law-- was this the husband of--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my brother-in-law, Kwashnieski, Isaac Kwashnieski.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So he--

speaker: interviewee
text: The husband of my sister, Frida.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Kwashnieski was with you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kwashnieski was not with me. He was in barrack B--

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that, but he was in Skarzysko-Kamienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: But to finish him off, they-- they send them to barrack C. And on that the same hall over there, a Polish foreman finished him off. He beat him up so badly, he didn't work as fast as they wanted, and he died over there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was bad, and I couldn't even help him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was-- he was working a different place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And neighbors, I was told that he-- that they beat him up so badly he couldn't even move. They take-- had to take him out. He was already, I think, bad-- dead at that time. Later on I found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were in Skarzysko-Kamienna for approximately two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, did-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did you have-- did the Jews have the opportunity to observe Jewish holidays at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not at all, even on Yom Kippur we had-- they were watching. They knew, some of them knew already that it's a Jewish holiday. They were watching us, and we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No Jewish holiday observation, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any prayer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope. Prayers-- I would like to bring one at this. I heard that one pair of tefillin were brought in to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were just a few people who-- who were putting on these tefillin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one was a friend of mine, and he was-- he's still alive. And I don't know if he knows that I know about it. I found out that he was one of them. He's the editor of the [NON-ENGLISH]. He was one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never witnessed it, but you heard about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never witnessed it, because this was strictly-- if they-- if the Germans will find out, they will kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out from people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who saw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, what it is, I-- I had contact with people that they were once-- there were some very fine, intelligent people over there, too. And we sat down once when we were off and we were talking about certain things, until somebody just told me a secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then, when you were in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- yes, in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were aware that some people were putting on tefillin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, putting on tefillin. They were very dangerous [INAUDIBLE], and he was once-- he was one of them that he put on-- he was a former [NON-ENGLISH]-- son of a yeshiva. Later on, he became a Jewish writer. And later on, he saw-- he was very young and it was in early-- so he was, at that time, about 22. And he was-- he was one of them who did it. And he is today's editor of the

speaker: interviewee
text: So how did I find out that he was a writer? In 1944, we had already a relaxation because the-- the Germans saw what was going on. The eastern front with the Russians, they knocked them out from Stalingrad and they-- they had downfalls, one after the other. So the Germans already-- looked already-- you know, they gave in a little. As a matter of fact, I had with one-- Meister [? Walter ?], he came to me. There was a few Jewish people, and one policeman. And he came to me-- he knew me already for some times, and he said, Kaminski, tell me. What are you going to do with me after the war? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And everybody was [? stone, ?] you know. And the policeman, the Jewish policeman, was also-- he couldn't-- nobody-- I didn't say a word. I says, what? All of a sudden-- let-- let the war get finished already. Then we'll see. And after that, the policeman notified our four-- our commandant, Eisenberg, that [? Walter ?], Meister [? Walter ?], the German, asked me that question. The commandant came to me, he says, what? All of a sudden you are such a big shot that you are talking to Meister-- to Meister [? Walter ?] about finishing the war, [INAUDIBLE]. I said, I didn't say anything. He just asked me the question what I will do with him after the war, that's all. Oh, yeah? Because I heard something different. No, that's the honest truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, what I'm trying to say is that everybody saw the end of the Germans is coming soon. The Russians are nearing to-- towards Poland. They had already part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were aware of this? This--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was aware. The Polish sadists, the Polish foremen who behaved like animals all of a sudden became like human beings. Especially one guy who had-- who behaved like a real animal. He all of a sudden--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember his name. And he came into the barracks, and he was so nice you couldn't believe. And I saw, a year before, 1942 and 1943, the way he behaved with the Jewish men, to the Jewish prisoners. Everybody-- and a lot of them, they were aware what's going on. It's the end of it. As a matter of fact, that-- it was on August 15th. They called everybody, and they decided that we're going to leave. But that night-- at that night, when we were supposed to-- they're supposed to transfer us-- where, we didn't know-- an organized group of about 60 or 70 people organized an organized escape. With the commandant Eisenberg, with the Mrs. Markovich-- [? Markevich ?]. She was also the second in command. And they escaped with policeman, with people that they were in contact with the Warsaw underground. We had people over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came from Warsaw, from [INAUDIBLE], and-- and-- and they had contact. They received some money also from them, and they escaped. I was-- this was at night. What happened? Later on, the escape was successful. Everything was OK. But later on, we went away. We didn't know what happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We found out later that there is-- that they were talking that they were killed. Where and when, we didn't know. Only after the war I found one policeman-- Jewish policeman over there-- in Israel. All of a sudden, you are alive? You are one of them that escaped that time from the camp. What happened to you? So he told me the whole story. These people ran away. They escaped with our [? mine. ?] We will escape to the mountains and join the Polish underground. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The people, they were walking. They couldn't ride because there was a lot of people over there, walking only at night. They came to their destination at the mountains. Svento Christi Gori, they call them. The name of the-- The holy mountains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Holy-- yes. The holy mountains over there. And that's where the place where the Polish gover-- the Polish underground, the army Krajowa and the army [INAUDIBLE] had their place, and they were hiding over there in the mountains. And that's where they camp. The man told me this after the war. Everything was fine, but one thing-- I had some kind of trouble with my foot from walking so much. And the mother of [? Markevich ?] and another three people, four people, they were behind. And they came over there and they came to the Polish underground. And they said, all right. We escaped from the camp. We would like to join the Polish underground to fight the common enemy, the Nazi-- Nazi Germany. They thought, well, if they were ready to fight, that they will be accepted. They were shot right away, one by one, by the Polish underground army Krajowa, [NON-ENGLISH], the initials of the army of Krajowa. They were shot. But the five people that they were behind-- they found out what happened, and they didn't even go any further. And that's what he told me. I forgot his name, and I-- and he told me that what happened to the 60 people what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who escaped that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: --From Skarzysko-Kamienna in the end of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1944. But we-- they took us about the 13th or 14th of August, 1944. 15Th August, we were all-- they were a selection. They called everybody in a selection, made whoever's healthy, good looking, one side, sick people on the other side. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they had quite a few people-- whoever was weak and didn't look well-- they took them that time to the-- to the firing squad and they killed them all. Us, the majority of the people from [INAUDIBLE], we were put into cattle cars, closed in, and they were taking us away. Where, we didn't know. We were there in the cattle cars, locked in, without food, without water, for five days. Every once in a while, they handed-- they-- they gave-- they threw in some bread. But I couldn't eat without water. I found out that it's more important-- water is more important than food. You couldn't-- you can live without food, even, for a few days. But without water, it was very, very, extremely hard. My lips were black and I felt so badly, and I got sick. And I didn't feel well because I didn't have any water and I couldn't eat. My brother felt much better. And after-- there were-- there were-- over there, it was terrible in the cattle cars. We were about 75 or 80 people with a pail of our necessities, you know, to relieve ourselves. And one-- especially one very fine, intelligent person-- some people got sick, mentally sick in-- and in this because--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were men and women at this point, or just men? Only men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Women was over there, too--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the different car?

speaker: interviewee
text: In different-- in different cattle cars. But for some reason, the women over there were in front, and the-- and the cattle cars in the back never got any-- any-- any water. Once I had a bad experience. We gave one-- it-- it-- he was a Russian or a Ukraine-- we paid him $100, he should bring us a bottle of water. He took the $100-- it was 100 zloty-- and he took the [INAUDIBLE] and he threw away just the bottle. Then we tried again. We had to do something. The other one was much nicer than him, and he brought us a bottle of water. But if we would have to share with all-- with some people who paid that money, it came out just we could just have a little-- a little drink. That I could have the whole bottle myself. Anyway, for five days we suffered, and we came to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for those five days that you were on the cattle car, they gave you no food, practically?

speaker: interviewee
text: They threw in some bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave you no water, but you had some--

speaker: interviewee
text: Water-- some other cattle cars, they received over there. But for some reason--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were deprived.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- ours-- and I-- there were another-- other cars like that, we were deprived of any water.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was limited contact with local people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said-- can you explain, you gave zloty to a Polish person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to a Polish person, to a Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Ukrainian, who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we, the Jewish people, there were some people that had a few-- a few-- some-- some money from before. They used to sell things, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainians were watching us. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So along the way they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The rifles, you know, watching us alongside. They had their own-- their own car, you know? And they were watching us, which nobody should escape. And the-- and the doors were a little open, about one foot open, and with wires, wired so that nobody can jump out and close, and nobody can break the [INAUDIBLE] wire and jump out. Finally we came to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said for the bodily functions there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: For bodily functions was only a pail.

speaker: interviewer
text: A pail. Did people perish along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Along the way they got sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they survived for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They survived. I don't remember of any-- this-- people went crazy, mentally sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what ha-- how did that manifest itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they did a lot of things and they were talking-- you know-- things that, you know, that they were-- they were sick, mentally. They got cra-- crazy altogether. We came to Buchenwald. We found out-- as soon as we passed Weimar, we found out that we are going to Buchenwald. In Buchenwald, we were afraid even. If they are not taking us, we didn't know how Buchenwald was. If they have also gas chambers, and they're gassing or not. We didn't know. We were still-- especially I was afraid, because I was sick that time. I came up, my brother supported me. [SOBBING] I was very sick at that time. I thought that it was finished. [CRYING] We didn't know-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we didn't know what's awaiting us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brother supported me. And we were going through. A doctor, they checked us. When he saw-- I was not shaved that time, and l looked bad. All black, my lips and all-- I-- I-- I looked all dried out, dehydrated completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me, do you feel all right? I didn't know how they are, if they are killing sick people. I said, oh, no. I'm OK, I'm all right. Oh, yeah? OK. So he let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, if I would say that I'm sick, what they would do. But that time, I found out before we went in, we wanted to find out if they had any gas chambers, if they are killing people. He said-- somebody told us, no. They are not killing. Not killing. Lately, they don't kill any-- anybody. They need people, as a matter of fact. Anyway, we went this, we went to-- we took a-- a shower. They gave us clothes, special clothes that the [GERMAN], that the prisoners were wearing, special clothes with [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and after a few days I came to myself-- my brother took care of me-- and I felt much better. We were over there just a while, a short while, in Buchenwald. I believe only two weeks. Then they sent us what they called aussenkommando. Aussenkommando, they send us to a place which is called Schlieben. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Schlieben. Over there was also HASAG, the same place as in Skarzysko. The same company owned it, and we-- and they were producing also armaments for the army over there in that place, in that Schlieben.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the company that owned it?

speaker: interviewee
text: HASAG.

speaker: interviewer
text: HASAG.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called it HASAG, Hugo Schneider and Company, they used to call it. These HASAG people, they own-- they took over almost all the weapon factories from Poland that they were in existence. They didn't pay a penny. They paid for each prisoner-- each slave prisoner-- they paid about four zloty to the SS. But they didn't care-- they-- they didn't mind if we are not getting any food. And they were really responsible I heard that, after the war, they didn't even sue the family who own the HASAG. But what I heard, I read later on that the owner, [? Boudin ?], I think was his name, and his wife committed suicide or it was just an explosion in one of their factories and they were killed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, we came over there. The atmosphere was entirely different in [INAUDIBLE] that we had. The Germans saw what was going on, that the end is nearing. And we were over there from a whole-- a whole winter over there, from August-- end of August to April 15th, working in ammunition factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was much, much-- number one, the soup, the food that we got was better. It wasn't the watery soup. And it was much better over there. The-- the-- the-- what do you call it? The bil-- the man who was responsible for our place was a very nice man. What do-- I forgot the name, [INAUDIBLE] his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, it was different because no such thing could be heard-- what I would like-- one-- one man died over there, and they allowed the son to go be-- beyond just the camp and bury him whichever they felt like. This we couldn't think, even, in barrack C in Skarzysko. The only thing there, they were killing and the family didn't even know what they did with and how he died, how the people died over there. And-- and like I said, we were already entirely different. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't have-- one thing we didn't have was the Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE], that we suffered mostly from them. And number two, we didn't have the sadists, the Polish foremens, that they-- that they were beating us up always. And this was-- whatever they had, all the Germans, that they were-- that they were working in the factories in Skarzysko, in Skarzysk, they brought over there. And instead of being big shots over there in Skarzysk, they gave them-- the leader of [INAUDIBLE] was a cripple. [INAUDIBLE], whatever his name was. Over there, they gave him only about five people. You should go and watch them, how they work over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they saw-- the beatings stopped over there. The beating for nothing. Anyway, this way they-- they were working. Once it was an explosion, a sabotage act. Who did the sabotage act? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a lot of Jewish people-- Jewish prisoners-- were wounded and died under this-- due-- due to the sabotage act. And over there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over there, I had much better. I was working in the barracks with my brother. And once it neared spring time, I would like to bring out a fact out of this the atmosphere was there. I had to do some work in the kitchen, and after I was finished, I knew that Passover-- the holiday of Passover-- was nearing. It was a few days before the Passover holiday. I says, I would like to get something from the kitchen, but I don't know. I'm afraid to risk to ask him, even.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this-- the chi-- the chef, German also-- was over there. And I asked him. I said to myself, let me try. The only thing he can say, he doesn't want to give me. And that's all. I said, would you please give me-- I need a couple pounds of white flour. Could you give me? He said, how many? Yeah, sure, I can give you. I said whatever you can, I would appreciate. And he gave me a couple pounds of white flour. And this was-- I was very happy-- and this was, like I said, it was just before Passover. I says, what am I going to do with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm going to bake. I would love to bake matzo for Pesach. And I did it myself. I saw the way my mother used to take-- make cakes or something. And I learned how-- what you need. You don't really need anything except water for matzo. And I said, I will make matzo for Pesach. Nobody-- I didn't ask anybody. And we had a little-- a little iron-- cast iron-- a little oven in each barrack to warm up. And I says, over there I'll put on-- maybe I can do the matzo over there. I made, with water and everything else, and I fix it up, and I made small little matzos, about two, three inches in diameter. And I made for 20 people, for all the people that we are in the-- in the-- in our barrack. And for Passover, when it was over there, I made a matzo and people were very, very happy. They said, now we can celebrate Passover in concen-- concentration camp.
